kjv_text: They part my garments among them, and cast lots upon my vesture.

thread.thread: Psalm 22:18, written ~1000 years early, names TWO acts a dying man's executioners would do — divide his garments AND cast lots for them; John 19:24 records the soldiers at Jesus' cross doing exactly both and cites this verse as fulfilled.
thread.anchor_detail: Roman soldiers at the foot of the cross dividing Jesus' garments among them and casting lots (rolling dice) for the seamless coat
thread.why_fresh: the undisputed garments detail — two distinct acts, both fulfilled to the letter — written a thousand years before a Roman cross by David, who was never executed
thread.gospel_landing: the precision proves the cross was the plan, not an accident — the suffering man is Jesus, laying down His life to win you back; come to Him
thread.rationale: the airtight, NT-cited cross-ref (no contested word)

beats.text: Ten centuries before the cross, a song recorded exactly how a dying man's clothes would be divided up.
Psalm twenty-two. David wrote it in the first person — but David himself was never executed; he died an old man. He was describing someone else: stripped, surrounded, his life poured out.

Watch one line — he wrote that the dying man's killers would divide his clothes, and cast lots for them: "They part my garments among them, and cast lots upon my vesture." A thousand years later, John watched it at the cross — soldiers dividing Jesus' clothes, then casting lots for the seamless coat — "that the scripture might be fulfilled".
A thousand years early, God had it written down — so when it happened, you'd know the cross was no accident. It was the plan.
The suffering man of Psalm twenty-two has a name: Jesus. They rolled dice for the clothes off His back, never seeing what He was really doing — laying down His life to win you back.
